section.title: Restore Us, O God
section.sectionRationale: An Asaphite lament for the Northern Kingdom, likely prayed as Assyrian threat pressed on Ephraim, Benjamin, and Manasseh. A rising threefold refrain, 'restore us,' frames a shift from shepherd to vine imagery, climaxing in a plea for 'the son of man' at God's right hand.
section.thesis: When God's own vine lies broken down and ravaged, His people's only hope is not their own strength but God's face shining again, embodied at last in the Son of Man at His right hand, upon whom restoration finally rests.
section.frameNote: Four movements, each intensifying the divine title in the refrain: the cry to the Shepherd of Israel, 1-3; lament over God's anger, 4-7; the vine from Egypt now ravaged, 8-13; and the plea for the son of man at God's right hand, 14-19.

units.label: Hear us, O Shepherd of Israel
units.note: the cry to the enthroned Shepherd

verses.text: Hear us, O Shepherd of Israel, who leads Joseph like a flock; You who sit enthroned between the cherubim, shine forth
before Ephraim, Benjamin, and Manasseh. Rally Your mighty power and come to save us.
{{shuv|Restore}} us, O God, and cause Your face to shine upon us, that we may be saved.

units.label: How long will Your anger smolder

verses.text: O LORD God of Hosts, how long will Your anger smolder against the prayers of Your people?
You fed them with the bread of tears and made them drink the full measure of their tears.
You make us contend with our neighbors; our enemies mock us.
Restore us, O God of Hosts, and cause Your face to shine upon us, that we may be saved.

units.label: The vine from Egypt, now ravaged

verses.text: You uprooted a {{gefen|vine}} from Egypt; You drove out the nations and transplanted it.
You cleared the ground for it, and it took root and filled the land.
The mountains were covered by its shade, and the mighty cedars with its branches.
It sent out its branches to the Sea, and its shoots toward the River.
Why have You broken down its walls, so that all who pass by pick its fruit?
The boar from the forest ravages it, and the creatures of the field feed upon it.

units.label: The son of man at Your right hand
units.note: the final, personal plea for restoration

verses.text: Return, O God of Hosts, we pray! Look down from heaven and see! Attend to this vine,
the root Your right hand has planted, the son You have raised up for Yourself.
Your vine has been cut down and burned; they perish at the rebuke of Your countenance.
Let Your hand be upon the man at Your right hand, on the {{benadam|son of man}} You have raised up for Yourself.
Then we will not turn away from You; revive us, and we will call on Your name.
Restore us, O LORD God of Hosts; cause Your face to shine upon us, that we may be saved.

shuv.gloss: To turn back, return, or restore. The refrain 'restore us' asks God to turn His people back to Himself and turn His own face back toward them, growing in intensity with each repetition, verses 3, 7, 19.

gefen.gloss: A vine or grapevine. Israel is pictured as a vine God Himself transplanted from Egypt and once caused to flourish over the whole land, an image Jesus later takes up directly of Himself, John 15:1.

benadam.gloss: Son of man, literally a human being. Here it names the man at God's right hand on whom restoration depends, language later charged with messianic meaning in Daniel 7:13 and taken by Jesus as His own title.

basic.context: Written for the northern tribes, Ephraim, Benjamin, and Manasseh, this psalm pictures God as Shepherd, then as the One who planted Israel like a vine out of Egypt. Now the vine is broken down, ravaged by wild boars, and the psalm's threefold refrain grows more urgent, restore us, cause Your face to shine, until it lands on a plea for the son of man at God's right hand.
basic.hermeneutics: The lament is real and historical, most likely from a period of northern crisis before Assyria's conquest, yet its climactic language, 'the man at Your right hand,' 'the son of man,' outgrows any single king and opens toward a fuller, messianic answer.
basic.originalLanguages: The refrain begs God to shuv, restore or turn back, His people and His own shining face. Israel is a gefen, vine, transplanted from Egypt, and the psalm's hope finally rests on ben adam, a son of man, at God's right hand, language Daniel 7:13 and the Gospels later take up directly of Christ.
basic.typology: The vine brought from Egypt and ravaged points forward to Jesus, the true vine, John 15:1, who succeeds where Israel failed. The 'son of man' raised up at God's right hand anticipates Christ, exalted to God's right hand as the Danielic Son of Man, Acts 5:31.
basic.christ: Jesus takes up the vine imagery of this psalm directly, calling Himself 'the true vine,' John 15:1, and He is the Son of Man exalted to God's right hand, the very position and title this psalm longs to see filled, Daniel 7:13, Acts 5:31.
basic.god: God is both Shepherd and Vinedresser, the one who planted and once made Israel flourish, and also the one whose anger, though real, is not the last word; His people can appeal to His own character to restore what He Himself once caused to grow.
basic.crossReferences: John 15:1-5 shows Jesus as the true vine, the deeper fulfillment of Israel's calling. Daniel 7:13 supplies the messianic weight later attached to 'son of man.' Numbers 6:24-26 stands behind the refrain's plea for God's face to shine, the priestly blessing this psalm turns into prayer.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's rising cry for restoration, from Shepherd to vine to the son of man at God's right hand, finds its answer in Jesus, the true vine and the exalted Son of Man, in whom God's face finally and permanently shines on His people.

context.literary: A lament built on an intensifying threefold refrain, restore us and cause Your face to shine, verses 3, 7, 19, moving from shepherd imagery, 1-3, through lament, 4-7, to an extended vine metaphor, 8-16, and a final personal plea, 17-19.
context.historical: Likely composed for the Northern Kingdom between the divided monarchy and Assyria's conquest in 722 BC, when Ephraim, Benjamin, and Manasseh faced mounting external threat and needed to appeal to God as their true Shepherd and Vinedresser.
context.canonical: Part of the Asaphite collection lamenting national crisis, standing alongside Psalm 79's grief over Jerusalem, and supplying vine and shepherd imagery that the New Testament, especially John 15, picks up directly.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To plead for God's restoring presence for the threatened northern tribes, grounding the appeal in His past care as Shepherd and Vinedresser, and to press toward a coming figure, the son of man at His right hand, through whom that restoration would finally and fully come.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific historical crisis facing the northern tribes; it prescribes as an abiding pattern that God's people may appeal to His covenant character and past faithfulness when present circumstances seem to contradict His care.

debates.question: Does 'the man at Your right hand... the son of man' in verse 17 refer to a reigning Davidic king of the psalmist's own day, or does it point forward messianically?

views.label: Primarily the reigning king or the nation itself, personified as the one through whom God's covenant favor would be restored
views.case: The immediate context concerns national deliverance for specific tribes under real threat, and 'son of man' can simply mean 'a human being,' fitting a contemporary royal figure.

views.label: A deliberately open messianic title that later Scripture, especially Daniel 7:13 and the Gospels, fills with its fullest meaning in Christ
views.case: The language exceeds any single historical king's actual deliverance, and the New Testament applies 'Son of Man' and exaltation to God's right hand directly and repeatedly to Jesus.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the psalm looks for a human figure at God's right hand through whom restoration comes, a hope Scripture shows fulfilled beyond any merely historical king in Jesus Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The refrain pleads shuv, restore, asking God to turn His people and His shining face back toward them. Israel is described as a gefen, vine, carried out of Egypt and once flourishing under God's own hand. The psalm's climax rests hope on a ben adam, son of man, at God's right hand, language later filled out fully in Daniel and the Gospels.

typology.type: The vine brought out of Egypt and now ravaged, verses 8-16
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the true vine, John 15:1, who fulfills and succeeds where Israel as God's vine repeatedly failed.

typology.type: The son of man at God's right hand, verse 17
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Son of Man exalted to God's right hand as Prince and Savior, Daniel 7:13, Acts 5:31.

study.christ: Jesus directly takes up this psalm's central image, declaring Himself 'the true vine,' John 15:1, the flourishing, faithful vine Israel was meant to be. He is also the Son of Man raised to God's right hand, the very figure and position this psalm's climactic plea longs to see established, language Daniel 7:13 develops and the apostles apply to the risen and exalted Christ, Acts 5:31.
study.god: God is Shepherd and Vinedresser both, the one who planted, cleared ground for, and once made His people flourish, whose anger against covenant unfaithfulness is real yet whose restoring face His people can still appeal to on the ground of His own past, covenant-keeping character.

crossReferences.note: Jesus identifies Himself as the true vine, fulfilling and succeeding where Israel as God's vine failed.
Supplies the messianic depth later attached to the psalm's 'son of man' at God's right hand.
The priestly blessing that stands behind this psalm's repeated plea for God's face to shine.

study.oneStory: Psalm 80's rising cry for restoration moves from Shepherd to vine to a plea for the son of man at God's right hand, and each image finds its full answer in Jesus, the true vine and the exalted Son of Man, in whom God's shining face rests permanently on His people.

quiz.q: How does the psalm address God in its opening verses, 1-2?

options.text: As the Shepherd of Israel, enthroned between the cherubim, who leads His people like a flock.
options.note: Verse 1 opens with exactly this shepherd and enthroned imagery.

options.text: As a distant, uninvolved observer of Israel's troubles.
options.note: The psalm addresses God as intimately involved, not distant.

options.text: As a warrior with no relationship to Israel's tribes.
options.note: God's mighty power is invoked, verse 2, but within a shepherd-and-people relationship, not detachment.

options.text: As one of several gods Israel worshiped.
options.note: The psalm addresses the one true God exclusively, not one among several.

quiz.q: What has happened to the vine God brought out of Egypt, verses 12-13?

options.text: Its walls have been broken down, so that passersby pick its fruit and wild boars ravage it.
options.note: Verses 12-13 describe exactly this devastation.

options.text: It has grown to cover the whole earth without any trouble.
options.note: The psalm describes the vine's earlier flourishing, verses 9-11, but now its present ruin.

options.text: It was never planted at all.
options.note: Verses 8-11 describe God planting and establishing the vine.

options.text: It has been carefully replanted in a safer location.
options.note: The text describes destruction and vulnerability, not relocation.

quiz.q: What does the psalmist ask God to place His hand upon, in verse 17?

options.text: The man at God's right hand, the son of man He has raised up for Himself.
options.note: Verse 17 makes this specific request.

options.text: A newly built temple.
options.note: No temple building is mentioned in this request.

options.text: The armies of the surrounding nations.
options.note: The request concerns a specific man at God's right hand, not foreign armies.

options.text: The boundaries of the land itself.
options.note: The request is personal, about a man at God's right hand, not territorial boundaries.

quiz.q: What is the psalm's repeated refrain, appearing in verses 3, 7, and 19?

options.text: "Restore us, O God, and cause Your face to shine upon us, that we may be saved."
options.note: This exact refrain repeats three times with intensifying divine titles.

options.text: "We will never call on Your name again."
options.note: The refrain is a plea for restoration, not a rejection of God.

options.text: "Our own strength will save us."
options.note: The refrain credits God alone with the power to save, not human strength.

options.text: "The nations will never trouble us again."
options.note: The refrain is directed to God's restoring action, not a claim about future peace from enemies.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's imagery connect to the New Testament?

options.text: Jesus calls Himself the true vine, and He is the Son of Man exalted to God's right hand, fulfilling both central images.
options.note: John 15:1 and Daniel 7:13 with Acts 5:31 supply these direct connections.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament at all.
options.note: The vine and son of man imagery are picked up directly by Jesus and the apostles.

options.text: It only refers to a literal, historical vineyard with no further meaning.
options.note: The psalm uses the vine as a metaphor for Israel, which the New Testament develops further in Christ.

options.text: It predicts the precise date of Jerusalem's rebuilding.
options.note: The psalm's imagery concerns restoration and a coming figure, not a rebuilding timeline.

questions: The psalm pictures Israel as a vine God Himself planted and once caused to flourish, verses 8-11. Where in your life has God clearly done the planting and growing, and where do you need to trust Him with the ravaged places?
The refrain 'restore us, cause Your face to shine' repeats and intensifies through the psalm. What situation are you tempted to give up praying about, when this psalm calls you to keep pleading?

The psalm names real anger and real tears, 'the bread of tears,' verse 5, without pretending everything is fine. Where do you need to bring honest lament to God rather than a performance of composure?
The psalm's hope finally rests on 'the son of man' at God's right hand, verse 17, fulfilled in Jesus. How does knowing Christ already holds that place change how you pray for restoration today?

The people promise, 'we will not turn away from You... we will call on Your name,' verse 18, once revived. What would renewed devotion to God look like for you this week, in light of what He has already done in Christ?

translationNotes.note: 'Shepherd of Israel' and 'enthroned between the cherubim' both recall the ark of the covenant, where God's presence was pictured as dwelling among His people.
'The Sea' refers to the Mediterranean, and 'the River' to the Euphrates, marking the fullest extent of the land promise, Genesis 15:18, that the flourishing vine once filled.

'The son of man' (ben adam) simply means a human being in ordinary Hebrew usage, yet its placement at God's right hand gave later readers, including Daniel 7:13 and the Gospels, grounds to hear a messianic hope here.

No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) could be confirmed specifically on Psalm 80 this session, so sermon status is 'none'.
